name: Mundaka Upanishad
summary: The Mundaka Upanishad distinguishes between the lower knowledge (apara vidya — sciences, rituals) and the higher knowledge (para vidya — knowledge of Brahman). It teaches that Brahman is the source of all, like sparks from fire, and can only be known through a qualified teacher.

verses.sanskrit: द्वे विद्ये वेदितव्ये इति ह स्म यद्ब्रह्मविदो वदन्ति परा चैवापरा च ||१.१.४||
verses.transliteration: dve vidye veditavye iti ha sma yad brahmavido vadanti parā caivāparā ca ||1.1.4||
verses.english_translation: Two kinds of knowledge are to be known — so say the knowers of Brahman — the higher and the lower.
verses.shankara_bhashya_english: Shankara: The higher knowledge is the direct knowledge of Brahman. The lower knowledge comprises the four Vedas along with their six auxiliaries — phonetics, ritual, grammar, etymology, metre, and astronomy.

verses.sanskrit:
यथा सुदीप्तात् पावकाद्विस्फुलिङ्गाः सहस्रशः प्रभवन्ते सरूपाः |
तथाक्षरात् विविधाः सोम्य भावाः प्रजायन्ते तत्र चैवापि यन्ति ||२.१.१||
verses.transliteration:
yathā sudīptāt pāvakād visphuliṅgāḥ sahasraśaḥ prabhavante sarūpāḥ |
tathākṣarāt vividhāḥ somya bhāvāḥ prajāyante tatra caivāpi yanti ||2.1.1||
verses.english_translation: As from a blazing fire thousands of sparks of the same nature leap forth, so from the Imperishable, O dear one, various beings arise and return there.
verses.shankara_bhashya_sanskrit: सुदीप्तात् पावकात् — प्रज्वलितात् अग्नेः — विस्फुलिङ्गाः — अग्निसरूपाः — प्रभवन्ते। तथा अक्षरात् ब्रह्मणः विविधाः भावाः — जीवाः — प्रजायन्ते।
verses.shankara_bhashya_english: Shankara: Just as sparks of the same nature as fire arise from a blazing fire, so from the Imperishable Brahman, various beings — individual souls — arise. And just as sparks return to the fire, all beings ultimately return to Brahman.

verses.sanskrit:
द्वा सुपर्णा सयुजा सखाया समानं वृक्षं परिषस्वजाते |
तयोरन्यः पिप्पलं स्वाद्वत्त्यनश्नन्नन्यो अभिचाकशीति ||३.१.१||
verses.transliteration:
dvā suparṇā sayujā sakhāyā samānaṃ vṛkṣaṃ pariṣasvajāte |
tayor anyaḥ pippalaṃ svādv atty anaśnann anyo abhicākaśīti ||3.1.1||
verses.english_translation: Two birds, companions always united, cling to the same tree. One of them eats the sweet fruit; the other, not eating, simply watches.
verses.shankara_bhashya_sanskrit: द्वा सुपर्णौ — जीवपरमात्मानौ। एकः जीवः — संसारवृक्षे कर्मफलानि भुङ्क्ते। अन्यः परमात्मा — साक्षी — न भुङ्क्ते।
verses.shankara_bhashya_english: Shankara: The two birds are the individual soul (jiva) and the supreme Self (Paramatman). The jiva eats the fruits of karma on the tree of samsara. The Paramatman is the witness — it does not eat, does not act, but simply illumines.
